summary: This PR adds the ability to set an explicit multipart boundary via a user-supplied `Content-Type` header, introducing `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` in `_multipart.py` and wiring it into `encode_multipart_data` / `encode_request`. The change is useful but ships with several correctness, security, and test-coverage issues that should be addressed before merging: most critically, the boundary value is not sanitised before being spliced into the wire body and `Content-Type` header (CRLF-injection risk), and the parsing logic can produce a boundary string with trailing whitespace, causing the declared and wire boundaries to diverge.

findings.title: Invalid content-type header path is untested
findings.detail: The branch that raises a `ValueError` when `headers` contains a `content-type` that does not start with `multipart/form-data` (e.g. `application/json`) has no test coverage. The error message and raise are completely uncovered, so regressions to this guard would go undetected.

findings.title: Boundary extraction does not strip trailing whitespace/garbage, causing declared and wire boundary to diverge
findings.detail: The parser uses `section.strip().split('boundary=')[-1]` without stripping the result. For a `Content-Type` value like `multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;`, the section `" boundary=+++ "` strips to `"boundary=+++ "`, and splitting on `"boundary="` then taking `[-1]` yields `"+++ "` (trailing space included). The extracted boundary is therefore encoded as `b"+++ "` (with a trailing space), while `_prepare` in `_models.py` uses `setdefault` so the caller's original header (declaring `boundary=+++`) is preserved unchanged. The wire boundary and the `Content-Type` declared boundary thus disagree, corrupting the multipart body. The fix is to `.strip()` the result of the split before returning it.

findings.title: Unvalidated boundary value enables CRLF/header injection
findings.detail:
The extracted boundary string is returned from `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` without any character-set validation. It is then (a) spliced verbatim into the multipart wire body as `b'--%s
' % self.boundary` and `b'--%s--
' % self.boundary`, and (b) interpolated directly into the `Content-Type` string at `MultipartStream.__init__` via `'multipart/form-data; boundary=%s' % boundary.decode('ascii')`. A boundary value containing `
` characters (e.g. `foo
X-Injected: bar`) will forge fake MIME parts in the body and inject arbitrary headers. RFC 2046 §5.1.1 restricts boundary characters to a safe printable-ASCII set with no CRLF. The extracted value must be validated (and rejected or escaped) against this character set before use.

findings.title: Case-sensitive header lookup and over-broad `startswith` check on Content-Type
findings.detail: Two related issues: (1) The lookup `"content-type" in headers` and `headers["content-type"]` assumes the caller's `Mapping[str, str]` normalises keys to lowercase. This works when `headers` is an httpx `Headers` object (which does case-insensitive lookup), but silently fails for a plain dict with `"Content-Type"` as the key — the boundary would never be extracted. The function's public signature advertises `Mapping[str, str]` but relies on case-insensitive behaviour. (2) The guard `content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data')` also accepts values like `multipart/form-data-evil` or `multipart/form-data.x-custom`. Prefer `content_type.split(';')[0].strip() == 'multipart/form-data'` for an exact media-type comparison.

findings.title: Boundary value extracted with `split('boundary=')[-1]` is incorrect for boundary values containing the substring 'boundary='
findings.detail: Using `.split('boundary=')[-1]` has two problems: (1) if the boundary value itself contains `boundary=` (e.g. `boundary=boundary=foo`), the last split token is returned (`foo`) rather than the full value (`boundary=foo`); (2) using `[-1]` instead of `[1]` means any additional occurrence of `boundary=` anywhere in the section produces an unexpected result. The cleaner approaches are `section.strip()[len('boundary='):]` (removing the known prefix) or `section.strip().partition('boundary=')[2]`, both of which are explicit and unambiguous. Alternatively, `split('boundary=', 1)[1]` limits the split to one occurrence.

findings.title: Redundant outer `if ";" in content_type` guard before `split`
findings.detail: The check `if ";" in content_type:` before the `for section in content_type.split(";"):` loop is unnecessary. When there is no semicolon, `split(";")` returns a one-element list and the loop iterates once, hitting the `ValueError` path correctly. The guard adds complexity without benefit and is inconsistent with simpler parsing patterns elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: No test for explicit `boundary` argument taking precedence over a `Content-Type` header
findings.detail: When both `boundary` (non-None) and a `headers` mapping with a `content-type` key are supplied, the `boundary is None` condition is False and the header-parsing branch is skipped. There is no test verifying that `boundary` takes precedence, so this interaction is untested.

findings.title: `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`: no-semicolon code path not unit-tested
findings.detail: A plain `'multipart/form-data'` string (no semicolon) immediately hits the `ValueError` raise without entering the loop. This path is never exercised as a direct unit test of this function; all existing tests pass content-type values that contain a semicolon.

findings.title: New `headers` parameter in `encode_request` / `Request` constructor not tested at unit level
findings.detail: The `headers` parameter added to `encode_multipart_data` / `encode_request` is exercised only through the high-level `Client.post` path. There are no direct unit-level tests of `encode_request(files=..., headers=...)` or of constructing a `Request` model directly with a multipart `content-type` header, making regressions in lower-level plumbing harder to catch.

findings.title: Truthy check on `headers` should use `is not None`
findings.detail: `if headers and boundary is None and ...` uses `headers` as a truthy check, silently skipping an explicitly passed empty mapping. The consistent pattern in this codebase is `if headers is not None`, matching how `boundary` itself is checked with `boundary is None`.

findings.title: Variable name `new_headers` is a workaround, not a meaningful name
findings.detail: The local variable was renamed to `new_headers` solely to avoid shadowing the new `headers` parameter, but `new_` carries no semantic meaning. A more descriptive name such as `content_headers` or `stream_headers` would be consistent with how other `encode_*` functions name this value.

findings.title: Dropped `boundary` keyword argument at `encode_request` call site may cause future confusion
findings.detail: The `encode_request` call in `Request.__init__` no longer passes `boundary=` explicitly. Even if this argument is currently unused at this call site, the omission is inconsistent with the function signature and may silently mislead future readers comparing the call site to the function definition.

findings.title: Inconsistent `parametrize` style across new and existing tests
findings.detail: Existing tests use `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), (...))` with outer parens, while the two new parametrize decorators (lines 45 and 75) use a plain string `"header"`. Pick one style consistently — either a bare string `"header"` or a single-element tuple `("header",)` — across all parametrized tests in this file.

findings.title: Boundary value includes trailing whitespace/garbage when a trailing semicolon or space is present
findings.detail: The parser does `section.strip().split('boundary=')[-1]`. For an input like `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;"`, splitting on `;` gives sections `["multipart/form-data", " boundary=+++ ", ""]`. The section `" boundary=+++ "` is stripped to `"boundary=+++ "`, which starts with `"boundary="`. Splitting that on `"boundary="` and taking `[-1]` gives `"+++ "` (note the trailing space). The boundary is therefore encoded as `b"+++ "` (with a trailing space), and `_prepare` uses `setdefault` so the caller-supplied `Content-Type` header is kept unchanged (with `boundary=+++`). The wire boundary and the declared boundary thus disagree, corrupting the request body.

findings.title: Boundary extraction uses `split('boundary=')[-1]` which is incorrect for boundary values containing the substring 'boundary='
findings.detail: The code uses `.split('boundary=')[-1]` to extract the boundary value. If the boundary value itself contained the string `boundary=` (e.g. `boundary=boundary=foo`), this would only return `foo` rather than `boundary=foo`. The correct approach is to split on `boundary=` at most once and take the second part, i.e. use `.split('boundary=', 1)[1]` instead of `.split('boundary=')[-1]`.

findings.title: Case-sensitive lookup on `headers` mapping may miss `Content-Type` set with different casing
findings.detail: The code checks `"content-type" in headers` and then does `headers["content-type"]`. The `headers` parameter is typed as `Mapping[str, str]`. If the caller passes `self.headers` (a `Headers` object that normalizes lookups to lowercase), this works. However, `encode_multipart_data` is a public-ish function accepting any `Mapping[str, str]`, and the documented convention in HTTP is that header names are case-insensitive. A caller passing `{"Content-Type": "..."}` would not match the `"content-type"` key lookup, so the boundary would never be extracted. In the `Request.__init__` flow `self.headers` is an httpx `Headers` object which does case-insensitive lookup, so this works in that specific path. But the function signature advertises `Mapping[str, str]` while relying on case-insensitive behaviour.

findings.title: When a user-supplied `content-type` boundary is found, `_prepare` will still overwrite it with `MultipartStream`'s auto-generated `Content-Type`
findings.detail: `encode_multipart_data` returns `new_headers = multipart.get_headers()`, which always contains a `Content-Type` built from the (correctly extracted) boundary inside `MultipartStream`. `_prepare` then calls `self.headers.setdefault(key, value)` for each key in `new_headers`. Because the user already set `Content-Type` on `self.headers` before `encode_request` is called, `setdefault` correctly skips overwriting it. However, the user-supplied header is, for example, `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++; charset=utf-8"` (with extra params), while `MultipartStream.content_type` is `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++"` (without extra params). The body is encoded with the correct boundary (`+++`), but the final `Content-Type` header retains all extra params the user supplied. This is fine **only if** the extracted boundary exactly matches what appears in the user header. The bug from finding #1 (trailing spaces not stripped from the extracted value) means the body boundary and declared boundary can diverge. This finding is closely linked to finding #1.

findings.title: Unvalidated boundary value enables multipart header injection
findings.detail:
The function `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` extracts the boundary token from the user-supplied `Content-Type` header and returns it without any validation. RFC 2046 restricts boundary characters to a safe set (printable ASCII excluding a handful of special chars, no whitespace or CRLF). Because the returned bytes are spliced verbatim into the body stream at `iter_chunks` (`b'--%s\r\n' % self.boundary` and `b'--%s--\r\n' % self.boundary`) and into the `Content-Type` header value at `MultipartStream.__init__` (`'multipart/form-data; boundary=%s' % boundary.decode('ascii')`), an attacker who can influence the `Content-Type` header (e.g. through an API wrapper that passes user-controlled headers) can:

1. **Inject CRLF sequences into the body**, crafting fake part boundaries or forging additional MIME parts – for example boundary `\r\n--injected\r\nContent-Disposition: form-data; name=evil` will break parsing on the receiving server.
2. **Inject arbitrary text into the `Content-Type` header** sent to the server, e.g. `; injected-param=value` or even CRLF-fold the header.

At a minimum the extracted boundary should be validated against the allowed character set defined in RFC 2046 §5.1.1 (`[A-Za-z0-9'()+_,-./:=? ]` with no leading/trailing spaces and no CRLF). A stricter approach is to percent-encode or reject anything outside that set and to strip surrounding whitespace before use.

findings.title: Boundary injected into Content-Type header without sanitisation allows header injection
findings.detail: In `MultipartStream.__init__`, the boundary is placed directly into the content-type string via `'multipart/form-data; boundary=%s' % boundary.decode('ascii')`. Because the boundary is taken verbatim from the user-supplied `Content-Type` header (via `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`) with no character filtering, a boundary containing `\r\n` characters (possible if the latin-1 decode in the extraction step produces them) would produce a malformed or injected HTTP header. For example, a `Content-Type` header like `multipart/form-data; boundary=foo\r\nX-Injected: bar` would propagate the injected header. Validate or sanitise the boundary value before this interpolation.

findings.title: Boundary extracted with split('boundary=')[-1] allows prefix bypass
findings.detail: The extraction logic uses `section.strip().split('boundary=')[-1]`. Using `[-1]` instead of `[1]` means that if a section contains `boundary=` more than once (e.g. `boundary=abc=boundary=evil`), the last occurrence wins. This is a minor parsing quirk that could produce an unexpected boundary value differing from what the server parses, but by itself is low impact. A cleaner approach is `section.strip()[len('boundary='):]` (i.e. remove the known prefix) to avoid ambiguity.

findings.title: Content-Type check uses startswith – bypassed by content types like 'multipart/form-data-extended'
findings.detail: The guard `content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data')` would also accept values like `multipart/form-data-evil` or `multipart/form-data.x-custom`. While this is an httpx client-side library (so impact is limited to the caller shooting themselves in the foot), it is a type-confusion avenue: the caller might supply a non-standard content type that passes the check but produces an invalid multipart request. Prefer an exact match on the media type token: `content_type.split(';')[0].strip() == 'multipart/form-data'`.

findings.title: Redundant outer `if` check before `split`
findings.detail: The outer `if ";" in content_type:` guard is unnecessary because the `for section in content_type.split(";")` loop already handles a string with no semicolons safely (it just iterates over one element). The guard adds complexity without benefit and is inconsistent with the simpler parsing style seen elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Use `partition` or direct split instead of `split(...)[-1]` to extract boundary value
findings.detail: `section.strip().split("boundary=")[-1]` would misparse a value that itself contained the literal string `"boundary="`. A cleaner and more explicit idiom consistent with the rest of the codebase would be `section.strip()[len("boundary="):]` or using `partition("boundary=")[2]`, making the intent obvious and avoiding accidental tail-splits.

findings.title: Truthy check on `headers` may silently skip an empty mapping
findings.detail: `if headers and boundary is None and "content-type" in headers:` — using `headers` as a truthy check means an explicitly passed empty `Mapping` is skipped silently. The existing codebase pattern for optional arguments is to check `if headers is not None`. This is consistent with how `boundary` itself is checked (`boundary is None`).

findings.title: Rename of `headers` to `new_headers` is a workaround, not a cleanup
findings.detail: The local variable was renamed from `headers` to `new_headers` purely to avoid shadowing the new `headers` parameter. A cleaner approach would be to choose a more descriptive name that reflects its meaning throughout the function (e.g., `content_headers` or `stream_headers`), consistent with how other `encode_*` functions name this value. The prefix `new_` carries no semantic meaning.

findings.title: Inconsistent `parametrize` style compared to surrounding tests
findings.detail: Line 91 uses `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), (...))` (unchanged, pre-existing code), but the two new parametrize decorators at lines 45 and 75 use `"header"` (a plain string, not a tuple). The existing tests in the file use `("key")` and `("value")` with outer parens. The new tests should use the same style — either `("header",)` or `"header"` — consistently with one or the other convention.

findings.title: The `boundary` keyword argument is silently dropped from the `encode_request` call
findings.detail: The `encode_request` call in `__init__` no longer passes `boundary=` explicitly. If `boundary` was previously passed through some code path into this call site, that argument is now missing. Even if `boundary` is not used at this call site currently, the omission is inconsistent with the function signature and could cause silent confusion for future readers comparing the call site with the function definition.

findings.title: Invalid content-type header path not tested
findings.detail: The branch at line 159–164 raises a ValueError when the content-type header is present but does NOT start with 'multipart/form-data' (e.g. 'application/json'). There is no test exercising this code path, so the error message and the ValueError raise are uncovered.

findings.title: No test for explicit boundary passed alongside a content-type header (both non-None)
findings.detail: When both `boundary` and `headers` containing a content-type are supplied together, the condition `boundary is None` is False and the header-parsing branch is skipped. There is no test verifying that the explicit `boundary` argument takes precedence over the header in this case.

findings.title: get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type: no-semicolon path not directly unit-tested
findings.detail: The function has a fast path: if there is no ';' in content_type it goes directly to the ValueError raise (line 30) without entering the loop. `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` uses headers that do contain ';', so that branch is covered, but a plain 'multipart/form-data' string (no semicolon at all) is never exercised as a direct unit test of this function.

findings.title: encode_request with headers parameter not tested in isolation
findings.detail: The `headers` parameter added to `encode_request` is only exercised via the high-level Client.post path. There are no direct unit-level tests of `encode_request(files=..., headers=...)` (analogous to the existing `test_multipart_encode*` tests that call `encode_request` directly), making it harder to catch regressions in lower-level plumbing.

findings.title: Request constructor path passing self.headers to encode_request not tested at model level
findings.detail: The changed block in `_models.py` now passes `self.headers` into `encode_request`. No test in `tests/models/test_requests.py` (or elsewhere at the Request-model level) exercises constructing a Request directly with a multipart content-type header to verify the new header-forwarding behaviour.

findings.title: Invalid content-type header path is untested
findings.detail: The branch that raises a `ValueError` when `headers` contains a `content-type` that does not start with `multipart/form-data` (e.g. `application/json`) has no test coverage. The error message and raise are completely uncovered, so regressions to this guard would go undetected.

findings.detail: The parser uses `section.strip().split('boundary=')[-1]` without stripping the result. For a `Content-Type` value like `multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;`, the section `" boundary=+++ "` strips to `"boundary=+++ "`, and splitting on `"boundary="` then taking `[-1]` yields `"+++ "` (trailing space included). findings.detail:
The extracted boundary string is returned from `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` without any character-set validation. It is then (a) spliced verbatim into the multipart wire body as `b'--%s
' % self.boundary` and `b'--%s--
' % self.boundary`, and (b) interpolated directly into the `Content-Type` string at `MultipartStream.__init__` via `'multipart/form-data; boundary=%s' % boundary.decode('ascii')`. A boundary value containing `

findings.detail: Two related issues: (1) The lookup `"content-type" in headers` and `headers["content-type"]` assumes the caller's `Mapping[str, str]` normalises keys to lowercase. findings.detail: When both `boundary` (non-None) and a `headers` mapping with a `content-type` key are supplied, the `boundary is None` condition is False and the header-parsing branch is skipped. There is no test verifying that `boundary` takes precedence, so this interaction is untested.

findings.detail: A plain `'multipart/form-data'` string (no semicolon) immediately hits the `ValueError` raise without entering the loop. This path is never exercised as a direct unit test of this function; all existing tests pass content-type values that contain a semicolon.

findings.detail: Existing tests use `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), (...))` with outer parens, while the two new parametrize decorators (lines 45 and 75) use a plain string `"header"`. Pick one style consistently — either a bare string `"header"` or a single-element tuple `("header",)` — across all parametrized tests in this file.
